transcript.transcript: My name is Tim Hackwith and I'm here today interviewing Laney Keller on the subject of mining. Okay, Laney, where are you from? Walla Sinavu. That's originally not right now, right? Right now I'm from Cordewa. Okay. How many mining camps are there around the Walls area? Right now there's two. Burt and Murray. I remember when I was young there used to be lots of them out there. There used to be one up by the Solaris, and by the Star, and by Burke, Murray, Pritchard, and up by Mullen there used to be one. Was there used to be one there called the Hercules Mine? Yeah, That was the one up by birth. How old were you when you first started mining? 16, unofficially. Is there a certain age you have to be to start? Yeah, you have to be 18 to be able to start mining. I started 16 because my dad is a foreman on the ground. Oh, so you could get underneath without anyone knowing. I rose down. Why is the reason for the 18-year-old mining? Because there's a child labor law that says no one can work in conditions like that until they're 18 and over. Oh, you don't believe me? How did they feel the first time you went under mine? What was it really like? Well, it was kind of scary because there's tons and tons of dirt on top there. If you come down at any moment and squash the life out of you, that would be the end of you. And it's dark. Everyone's got a bad feeling about being in mines because they're going to think, I'm squished in. But I've been in rooms, I mean what I call them rooms, I know where they have places that are bigger than this whole dorm room. You know, they're just big rooms. Do you have anybody that comes down there the first time and they're really super scared? Yeah, we had an air blast when this one guy came down first. An air blast is where a lot of air is being released from big pockets in the ground. And we were all in the lunchroom and the lunchroom exploded. And there was rocks falling all over the place. And that guy was hiding on the table with a dragon moth. What do you mean by lunchroom? Do you lunchrooms down in the mines? Oh yeah, we eat in the mines. Oh really? Yeah, there's little places set off with air doors and everything so it can't be contaminated. Set off from their well. What was the name of the first mine you ever went into? Dew Drop. Dew Drop Mine. That's my grandfather's. What's your grandfather's name by the way? Carl Taylor. What does it mean, the transportation you need to get down in this mine? To get down in it? Right. It's called a hoist or a bucket. How does that work? Well, it's got to have a big drum up on top with all this cable wrapped around it and hooked onto a, do you say, They're called decks, you know, like little boxes. And we can fit about 10 guys into each box. And they're stacked on top of each other to make a hoist or a bucket. And they're lowered from there down to each different level. See, when I went down, I remember they had, you know, you know, really cold, like the cold day. You need to be down here. It's really warm if you go down. Oh, yeah. In the bottom of Lucky Friday, they have a refrigerator. It's a refrigerated level. It's an experiment that they're doing. It's a really cool level. It's nice to work on because it's just normal temperature. You don't sweat as much. What's the safety like in that kind of a, like in that refrigerated one? What has the difference to it than a normal one? Oh, yeah. There's a lot more air locks, which are doors that are real tightly put together so no air can get in and control it. And they don't have a lot of holes leading to other levels, because they do all the cold air will leak out. There's a lot more safety. And they have airlocks at the beginning of each level. You know how, I don't know if you've been down there, but how most levels, when you just come down to them, you can walk right onto the level. These ones you have to pass through airlocks. And you have to be going pretty quick or you'll get caught in the airlock. So these ones open automatically and shut. They're not opened by you. They're opened by someone beyond that can see you through the windows in the airlock. Isn't that kind of dangerous in a situation where you've got like a KU or something? Oh yeah. That's just an experiment. There's only got a couple people on this level. There's like maybe ten guys on the whole level. Okay, when you're down there, What kind of tools do they use? Well, okay, when you start out, the basic tools that you use is a pick and a double jack, which is a fledge hammer. Those are the basic tools that you use when you first start out because they want you to get some experience before you go on and hire. And then you get to where... Well, how do you use those tools? I don't know, what are you talking about? How do you use those tools? Okay, you're standing on top of these big grates. Yeah. And they bring...well, you're not on the grates at this time. They bring rocks in and they pour them on top of the grate. And you have to either beat them through the grate or find a way to flip through it. And once you've been there long enough, you find ways of flipping it through without beating it. Oh, okay. What other tools do they use? They use machines, which are little motors that run drills, and jack legs, which are the same thing as a machine except they have a leg. They're hydraulic. Well, no, pneumatic. Yeah, and it's pushed up, and it pushes the machine up so you can drill up above you, high above you, so it just makes it easier on the liners. And then you have the mucker, which dredges a lot of the, takes all the muck from where you just blasted and pushes it down a chute to where the people can load it onto a low key to take it out to the station. Were these tools any different to the ones you used when you first started? Oh yeah, oh yeah. When I first started I was working, well, the first time I went down was with my grandfather and his mine. And he uses all old, old-timey tools. Nothing is used by air pressure or anything. We had to seat the drills into the wall with a fledge hammer. And each time that they hit, you had to turn the drill so it wouldn't stick. So you could pull it back out. Is that how they find like a vein? I mean, when you go about finding a vein, when I was down there before, they used a big long drill and you can drill back and then they pull out the whole core of it. Is that how they do it back there now? What, you mean just back in the old days? Well, yeah, now. Well, at the same time, now everybody's just going to go back and they show this, just go back and they drill. Well, now they've got it from the satellite. They've got satellite pictures of which way the vein runs. Oh, really? How do they do that? How can you get a picture of underneath the ground? I don't know. The satellite takes it and they show we have little veins poked up down in the ground. And you just start going back until you hit it. Huh. OK, do they use a lot of blasting in the mine? Yeah, they have regular blasting times. They blast three times a day, once for each ship. They have three ships. And the blasting times are like at 11, 3, and at midnight. They take all the men out of the mine when they do that, don't they? Uh-uh. They don't take any? Not at the Lucky Friday, right? Just have to be working. They move them out to the station, which is a long way away from any of the blasting and drilling that's going on. And that's where they load up. But I've been in other mines, like the Sunshine, where they do load everybody up and take them out before... Well, they load them up on the hoist, they trigger it from the hoist, and they bring them to go up. So they're on their way up when it's going up. Okay, do you ever know any of the processes that they do when they first saw the mine, the tunnel area? Yeah, why did you help me out with that? Well, the first thing we did is we had to start with, I don't know how they did it, but we used a bulldozer and dug in about 20 feet or so. Then we framed it all up with bulkheads and a lot of other wood for support, and some concrete at the very beginning. Then we started just like we did before, we drill and you blast. You put in more wood, you drill and you blast. You're working your way down. A lot of our different men, not really miners, but A lot of electricians. Yeah, a lot of electricians. A lot of electricians and engineers, which, by the way, no miner likes an electrician or engineer. Why is that? Because they're lazy. They just sit around. There's a lot of broken down equipment on there that they won't fit. Why would they fix it? Because it's their lunch break. But there's been a miner, I'll tell you that, electricians have lunch breaks all day long. So they really don't get along because they're out on lunch break. How many men work in one certain area of the mine? Well, like in the stokes, there's usually between two and three, because there's the partners, which are the guys that are drilling, and a third guy called the nippers, or the third man. And what he'll do is he'll run down and get supplies, or he'll muck, or sometimes he'll drill. He's just there to help if he gets in trouble. On a station, there can be 10 to, well, from 5 to 10 guys in the station at a time. That's the main part of the level where all the ore is dumped at. Right. And then there's, you can find two guys in the drift at all times, repairing it. Everyone works in pairs though, no one works alone. Because if you work alone you have a good chance of getting hurt and there's no way you can get out of your tail anymore. More like safety is priority. Yeah. You said before that you have these huge, when you shove the rock in, They go down to another platform there where they load it up. What do they load it up into? They go to the top of the horse. We just took it from them. They have a bucket up on top of the horse that they load it on into. How big are these buckets? They can pull out, I think, two tons in one bucket. They pull it all the way? What, do they pull it straight out? Yeah, they bring it up. Okay, when they get reloaded on, they just pull right up to the hoist where they were at, where they loaded all the men at. They bring it all the way up, and when it gets to the top, the hoist flips over, and it dumps it onto a conveyor belt, and it goes back down again. Sure. What was it like working in the mines with the old guys? I mean, the old miners were the worst. Oh. That was kind of funny, because they all had their own little stories and everything to tell. We had this one guy that lived in World War II and he was captured. And he used to tell us about how the Germans always make, you know, was really sad when the field guys did some stuff like that. But they do. They know a lot. You can really learn a lot from them. And they have different terms for the tools than the new miners. They call fuses, instead of calling them primers, they call them dope snakes. There's a lot of different old names for them. Oh yeah. Do any of these old pioneers know if they get cranky when down here from being an off-line, if they try to get out or anything? Well, it depends. There's some that think that they can still do their thing on their own. They're getting old and they can't laugh a lot, but they still do it. There's one thing, they don't believe that women should do that in the morning. Because in the old days, a woman went to the mine, even just for a tour, that mine was shut down because it was not that much to the miners. Do you have any more old tales like that you can tell me? The old tales you told me? Old tales? Tales. Yeah, the Danit Blanket probably didn't have a guy called the Galloping Ghost. He was riding on a... This is how he got killed. He was on a burrow. And a rock fell on top of him and squashed him up pretty bad. His book fails to hear him walking his burrow around. He was on a graveyard shift, so he can't hear any of the other shift. I don't know why. You can always hear him. I've been on graveyard shifts before and you can actually hear the hooves. They always talk about how back in the old days the mine shaft was never going straight down, it was slanted. And they used to ride the hoist down and it used to scrape along the side and it would come to a big open area where they'd blow it and the hoist would slip down in and you could get out like that and you'd have to jump out. And they used to have to climb up to it by ladders to the hoist because they couldn't get the hoist down close enough to the station to get in it. Are nowadays all the mines where you just walk right to a section? Yeah. Or are you still going down the ladder or anything like that? Oh, you still have to go down ladders. Well, I've done stuff where you have to climb 200 feet to get to a stove. That's tiring. You've got to go up by 14 ladders. I don't know. It's quite a way. You get really tired because it's hot. The air doesn't circulate too good. Some places way back in. Barstow on the east side of the Friday night. They pipe down all the areas, don't they? Yeah, it's all. They have to. Right. It's all took down in the bore and it circulated through the huge different fills and everything. It has patterns. That's what airlocks were built for. Because certain levels get air from certain places. So they got two, on a Friday they had two pumps. And One pumped in the top half of the mine and one pumped in the bottom half of the mine. Then they built a third one for the refrigerated level. It sounds like Christmas holiday is coming up. What are those Christmas like in the morning? Oh boy. They each, every level has a competition with the other ones to see which level they can, which level would be the prettiest, you know. And they hang up, they build a tree and they plant it in there. And they decorate it and they decorate all the Lokis and all the other carts that are down there. And everyone dresses up their helmets, and they color their lights green and red. And they clean it up quite a bit. It doesn't look like the same line if you're moving forward and they really... And then they have a big party the night before Christmas. All... Everyone's drunk, no one... No one does any work and... And you don't get kicked out because, you know, the line is just like a holiday. Miners literally don't even have to come, they just come and drink with all their friends. And they go down in the mine to have a party? Uh-huh. They do. Everyone gets together on one level. It's usually like 42, 50, because it's the biggest level. And everyone just gets down there. What's the deepest mine that you've ever been down? Sunshine. It's over a mile deep. It's quite a ways down. Is there any disaster that you've been able to co-captain with in the mine? Yeah, I was in a cavein. It took them three days to get me out. It was down in and the air blast went off and it destroyed the whole level. We were back in one of the stoves and our stove was caving in and we couldn't get out. Me and my partner were stuck in that cave and our nephro was stuck down just below us. We could talk to him but he couldn't come out fast enough. He couldn't get out from where he was at either. How does that feel to be in there like that? It's pretty scary. You can still breathe because the air is still coming through. That's like you've gone to sleep two times and nobody's coming, you don't even know if they're going to even worry about you. You don't even know if they realize a cave-in has happened. And if a cave-in has happened, What if the hoist can't come down? You're stuck. It is too beautiful. Yeah, it is. You may die. It makes you grow up fast. I bet. Is there any family-like atmosphere down there in the morning? Oh yeah. I was the smallest one down there so I was the little baby and everyone called me the little baby and everyone watched out for me. One time we were up above at a bar, of course I was drinking but it doesn't matter anyway as long as I'm there. We were at a bar and this guy came up who didn't like me from school. And he started pushing me around. There's this guy down there they call Shorty, who's not shorty, he's seven foot tall. And he's really big. And So he picked up the guy and he told him that he better leave me alone or he was going to get at about then to another minor step because all that guy's friends came over. They're putting their place. There's some pretty interesting guys down there. This guy that I met that was in Vietnam for like six years in the special forces. And he really knows a lot. That's the kind of self-defense process. And they had a golden glove walker from Las Vegas that worked down there. I guess it's interesting, yeah. I bet. All done. You built them up a little quick, man. Oh, yeah. Oh, yeah. Do you remember anything about the old way? I mean, things had to be different from now. Yeah, it takes a long time. It's just, you do all the things the same now, except for there's no motors at all. Everything was loaded in on burrows and out on burrows. And everything was done physical. Some of it was like a planted piece of wood. Right, You didn't drop down like you do now. And the girls did burrows and mules and all the work. As far as pulling and pushing it, just a lot of physical work. You have to do a lot of pounding and lifting. If you weren't in shape, that's why the miners used to be really big because they had to be to do all that. What kind of safety is there, especially in the mine? Safety for the eyes is a lot, is a big one. They gave you a special safety jacket that you have to wear. And if you're not found wearing it, you could be sitting in the mine for three days. Or if the federal safety inspector catches you, he can kick you out of the mine. It's sort of like blacklist, yeah. Make sure none of the other mines in the valley, up in the Silver Valley, take you out. Is there any special safety for like, you know, in a certain area, do you have to... Yeah, like, when you're working on the Grizzlies, Grizzlies is a great way, you have to pound all the rock through. You have to wear glasses, safety glasses, because all I know why, I've had my face cut off before from flying rockets when you're pounding on them. And you have to wear gloves, that's a big one, because I've seen people get their fingers cut off because they weren't wearing gloves. By cables. You just have to use a lot of common sense. And no running, that's for sure. Because that causes a lot of accidents. If you run, you just slip. Yeah. You probably couldn't dance on the mud holes. Oh yeah. Some levels are really dry, and others are just like muddled. I was on one where I almost had to swim. What were the safeties for, like, in your... What kind of safety devices did you get to wear? Like, We had to have a lamp. That was August because if you don't, like I see, we had self-rescuers. Which you're supposed to be able to know how to take apart, which most of mine just didn't, I found out when I got down there. And because we had to have like, it's like a fire field, you know, and how you get out and stuff. But anyways, you're supposed to be able to take them apart, and if you use them correctly, it'll take out all the carbon, you know, dioxide and monoxide and everything out of the air so you don't die but it gets really hot and burns your mouth. And you have to, like I said, safety glasses is the biggest thing. They make sure you have at least two pairs of earplugs because you can only take 90 decibels of noise for eight hours. If you take any more than 90 decibels, then you could be able to sue the company for not giving you earplugs. What was the longest shift you had to work down there? Longest shift? Most of the shifts were eight hours. And that's including the one hour off for lunch. Yeah, but like I said, I was trapped down there for three days. How long did it take you to get to work? I worked about 6 hours before we got trapped in and we were almost getting ready to come out. The longest I've been was I've been done with shift once because I went on work and my dad's partner didn't show up so I went and worked with my dad. What's the biggest thing that sticks out in your mind about mining? Well, you got to use common sense. If you're down there, you don't want to get hurt. You got to watch out for yourself all the time. You know, be careful. It's a good way to make a lot of money. You can make quite a bit. I make $250 a week and I'm only 18, starting out at second level. And just be careful or you're going to get hurt. What do you remember about the Big Sun sign disaster that happened a while back? I remember that there was a lot of miners killed. There was 90, something like 99. I think you needed one more to make it 100. And my uncle, I had two uncles, it says that if the fire would have started one shift later, and my two uncles would have been caught in the fire, and they would have been killed too. They had to go down to rescue, or they were supposed to go down to China to rescue everybody. They're the ones that found those two miners that were up in the shaft trying to get oxygen. And they said they would find all kinds of people down there that didn't know how to use their self-rescuers and that's why they died. And because of all the gases in the air they breathe the gas in and they kill them because if they found people it was really hard because they find their friends they knew you know and they were dead nothing they could do about it and they just wanted to cry or something but you had to take off your mask, you'd die. And they used a lot of liquid oxygen and stuff, you know. That was the first time I guess that was used. And they went down to try and save all those miners. They said they remember finding, I think it was 23 miners that bulkhead themselves in. That's when you put up a big wall in the middle of a shall to try and save yourself. And the company turned off all the air from the fans. And when they turned off the fans, all the gases got behind the bulkhead and killed the guys. If they wouldn't have turned off the fans, most likely the guys would still be alive today. So it's just little things like that that really made some of the guys kill themselves. Yeah, Just little stupid mistakes like turning off the oxygen and not knowing how to use your own self-rescuer. You know, that was like nine years ago and it seems to me that people still remember it because some of you guys died, but those guys down there don't know how to use their self-rescuer. Some of them don't even have self-rescuers on them. And that's the easy way to get yourself killed. It's gotten right away then, isn't it? Yeah, it pretty much is. And it's going to take a lot of disaster to make people think about it. I suppose that, you know, right after it happened a lot of people were really worried about it for a couple of years to follow. But last year when I was down here no one really cared. Like when we had our fire drill, some of the guys didn't even put on their self-rescues. They just walked out. What was the town like while you were coming here? What was it like? It was pretty bad because everyone's wondering if their father, their brother's dead, you know. And you could go up to the mine and you could see smoke pouring out of the shaft and the boreholes and everything else. And they tried to, my dad tried to lead an expedition down in through the Polaris site in the mine, which he drilled out, and to try and see if anyone came out that way and they found a couple guys that tried to come out that way. They were just wondering around. It's sad because when they let everyone out of school, I don't think it was really worth it. If anyone says, oh, good, we got a school bit. No one knew how anyone had died in the mines. It really wasn't worth it at all. Because how can a life compare to two weeks of school? Right. Well, thanks a lot, Lanie. All right. Well, thank you

lines.line: > The speaker introduces himself as Jim Hackworth and mentions that he is interviewing Lanny Keller about mining. Lanny Keller is from Wallace, Idaho but currently resides in Cortland. The interview sets the stage for a discussion about mining, particularly focusing on the mining camps around Wallace, Idaho.

metadata.title: Introduction to the Interview
metadata.synopsis: The speaker introduces himself as Jim Hackworth and mentions that he is interviewing Lanny Keller about mining. Lanny Keller is from Wallace, Idaho but currently resides in Cortland. The interview sets the stage for a discussion about mining, particularly focusing on the mining camps around Wallace, Idaho.

lines.line: > Lanny Keller started mining unofficially at the age of 16, despite the legal age being 18, due to his father's position as a foreman. He describes his initial experiences in the mines as scary, emphasizing the dangerous conditions and the fear associated with being underground.

metadata.title: Early Mining Experiences
metadata.synopsis: Lanny Keller started mining unofficially at the age of 16, despite the legal age being 18, due to his father's position as a foreman. He describes his initial experiences in the mines as scary, emphasizing the dangerous conditions and the fear associated with being underground.

lines.line: > Safety in mining is a critical concern, with strict regulations to prevent accidents. The speaker details various safety measures, including the use of airlocks and specialized equipment to maintain breathable air. He also recounts a personal experience of being trapped in a mine for three days due to a cave-in, highlighting the dangers miners face.

metadata.title: Mining Safety and Disaster Preparedness
metadata.synopsis: Safety in mining is a critical concern, with strict regulations to prevent accidents. The speaker details various safety measures, including the use of airlocks and specialized equipment to maintain breathable air. He also recounts a personal experience of being trapped in a mine for three days due to a cave-in, highlighting the dangers miners face.

lines.line: > The speaker discusses the evolution of mining tools from manual tools like picks and sledgehammers to more advanced pneumatic machines and drills. He explains how these tools were used in the mining process, including the techniques for breaking rocks and drilling.

metadata.title: Mining Tools and Techniques
metadata.synopsis: The speaker discusses the evolution of mining tools from manual tools like picks and sledgehammers to more advanced pneumatic machines and drills. He explains how these tools were used in the mining process, including the techniques for breaking rocks and drilling.

lines.line: > The speaker shares anecdotes about the older miners, describing their stories from past experiences like World War II and their knowledge of mining. He reflects on the camaraderie and protective nature of relationships among miners, illustrating how they look out for one another, especially in dangerous situations.

metadata.title: Social Dynamics in the Mines
metadata.synopsis: The speaker shares anecdotes about the older miners, describing their stories from past experiences like World War II and their knowledge of mining. He reflects on the camaraderie and protective nature of relationships among miners, illustrating how they look out for one another, especially in dangerous situations.

lines.line: > Mining is rich with traditions and superstitions, as illustrated by tales like the 'Galloping Ghost' of the Lucky Friday mine. These stories are part of the cultural fabric of mining communities, often reflecting their history and the challenging conditions they face.

metadata.title: Mining Traditions and Superstitions
metadata.synopsis: Mining is rich with traditions and superstitions, as illustrated by tales like the 'Galloping Ghost' of the Lucky Friday mine. These stories are part of the cultural fabric of mining communities, often reflecting their history and the challenging conditions they face.

lines.line: > The speaker discusses a significant mining disaster at the Sunshine mine, which resulted in the death of 99 miners. He describes the community's response, the emotional toll on the rescuers and families, and the importance of safety measures like proper use of self-rescuers to prevent such tragedies.

metadata.title: Impact of Major Mining Disasters
metadata.synopsis: The speaker discusses a significant mining disaster at the Sunshine mine, which resulted in the death of 99 miners. He describes the community's response, the emotional toll on the rescuers and families, and the importance of safety measures like proper use of self-rescuers to prevent such tragedies.
